exercises.title: In-Memory Database with CRUD
exercises.description: Simulate a database using a Python dict. Build a `UserDB` class with `create(name, email)`, `read(user_id)`, `update(user_id, **fields)`, `delete(user_id)`, and `list_all()` methods. Each user gets an auto-incrementing integer ID. Demonstrate all operations and print results.
exercises.starterCode:
class UserDB:
    def __init__(self):
        self._data = {}  # {id: user_dict}
        self._next_id = 1

    def create(self, name, email):
        # TODO: Create a new user dict with 'id', 'name', 'email'
        # Store it in self._data with self._next_id as key
        # Increment self._next_id
        # Return the new user dict
        pass

    def read(self, user_id):
        # TODO: Return the user dict for user_id, or None if not found
        pass

    def update(self, user_id, **fields):
        # TODO: Update the user's fields (only 'name' and 'email' allowed)
        # Return the updated user, or None if not found
        pass

    def delete(self, user_id):
        # TODO: Remove the user from _data
        # Return True if deleted, False if not found
        pass

    def list_all(self):
        # TODO: Return list of all users sorted by id
        pass

db = UserDB()

# Create users
alice = db.create("Alice", "alice@example.com")
bob = db.create("Bob", "bob@example.com")
carol = db.create("Carol", "carol@example.com")
print(f"Created: {alice['name']} (id={alice['id']})")
print(f"Created: {bob['name']} (id={bob['id']})")
print(f"Created: {carol['name']} (id={carol['id']})")

# Read
user = db.read(2)
print(f"Read id=2: {user['name']}")

# Update
updated = db.update(1, email="alice@newdomain.com")
print(f"Updated Alice's email: {updated['email']}")

# Delete
result = db.delete(2)
print(f"Deleted id=2: {result}")

# List all remaining
print("All users:")
for u in db.list_all():
    print(f"  {u['id']}: {u['name']} <{u['email']}>")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Created: Alice (id=1)
Created: Bob (id=2)
Created: Carol (id=3)
Read id=2: Bob
Updated Alice's email: alice@newdomain.com
Deleted id=2: True
All users:
  1: Alice <alice@newdomain.com>
  3: Carol <carol@example.com>
hints: In create(), build the user dict then do self._data[self._next_id] = user before incrementing.
In update(), get the user first; if found, update only the allowed keys from **fields.
In delete(), use self._data.pop(user_id, None) — returns None if not found.
In list_all(), use sorted(self._data.values(), key=lambda u: u['id']).

exercises.title: Query Filtering Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate database query filtering. Given a list of task records (dicts), write a `TaskQuery` class that supports chaining: `filter_by_status(status)`, `filter_by_user(user_id)`, `order_by(field)`, and `limit(n)`. Each method returns `self` for chaining. A final `execute()` method returns the filtered list.
exercises.starterCode:
TASKS = [
    {"id": 1, "title": "Buy groceries", "status": "done", "user_id": 1, "priority": 2},
    {"id": 2, "title": "Write report", "status": "pending", "user_id": 2, "priority": 1},
    {"id": 3, "title": "Call dentist", "status": "pending", "user_id": 1, "priority": 3},
    {"id": 4, "title": "Fix bug", "status": "in_progress", "user_id": 2, "priority": 1},
    {"id": 5, "title": "Read book", "status": "pending", "user_id": 1, "priority": 2},
    {"id": 6, "title": "Send invoice", "status": "done", "user_id": 2, "priority": 3},
]

class TaskQuery:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self._data = list(data)  # Work on a copy

    def filter_by_status(self, status):
        # TODO: Keep only tasks with matching status
        # Return self for chaining
        pass

    def filter_by_user(self, user_id):
        # TODO: Keep only tasks for the given user_id
        # Return self for chaining
        pass

    def order_by(self, field, ascending=True):
        # TODO: Sort self._data by the given field
        # Return self for chaining
        pass

    def limit(self, n):
        # TODO: Keep only the first n items
        # Return self for chaining
        pass

    def execute(self):
        return self._data

# Query 1: All pending tasks for user 1, ordered by priority
results = TaskQuery(TASKS).filter_by_user(1).filter_by_status("pending").order_by("priority").execute()
print("User 1 pending tasks:")
for t in results:
    print(f"  [{t['priority']}] {t['title']}")

# Query 2: Top 2 tasks by id (all users)
results = TaskQuery(TASKS).order_by("id", ascending=False).limit(2).execute()
print("Last 2 tasks by id:")
for t in results:
    print(f"  id={t['id']}: {t['title']}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
User 1 pending tasks:
  [2] Read book
  [3] Call dentist
Last 2 tasks by id:
  id=6: Send invoice
  id=5: Read book
hints: Each method should modify self._data and return self.
filter_by_status: self._data = [t for t in self._data if t['status'] == status]
order_by: self._data = sorted(self._data, key=lambda t: t[field], reverse=not ascending)

exercises.title: Relationship Simulation (One-to-Many)
exercises.description: Simulate a one-to-many database relationship. Build a `BlogDB` with users and posts stored in separate dicts. Add a `get_user_with_posts(user_id)` method that returns a user dict with an additional 'posts' key containing their posts. Also implement `count_posts_per_user()` that returns a dict of {user_name: post_count}.
exercises.starterCode:
class BlogDB:
    def __init__(self):
        self.users = {
            1: {"id": 1, "name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com"},
            2: {"id": 2, "name": "Bob", "email": "bob@example.com"},
        }
        self.posts = [
            {"id": 1, "title": "Intro to Python", "user_id": 1},
            {"id": 2, "title": "Flask Basics", "user_id": 1},
            {"id": 3, "title": "My First Post", "user_id": 2},
            {"id": 4, "title": "Advanced Decorators", "user_id": 1},
            {"id": 5, "title": "Testing Tips", "user_id": 2},
        ]

    def get_user_with_posts(self, user_id):
        # TODO: Get the user dict (copy it)
        # TODO: Add a 'posts' key with the list of their posts
        # Return None if user not found
        pass

    def count_posts_per_user(self):
        # TODO: Return {user_name: post_count} for all users
        pass

db = BlogDB()

# Get Alice with her posts
alice = db.get_user_with_posts(1)
print(f"User: {alice['name']}")
print(f"Post count: {len(alice['posts'])}")
for post in alice['posts']:
    print(f"  - {post['title']}")

# Count posts per user
counts = db.count_posts_per_user()
for name, count in sorted(counts.items()):
    print(f"{name}: {count} posts")
exercises.expectedOutput:
User: Alice
Post count: 3
  - Intro to Python
  - Flask Basics
  - Advanced Decorators
Alice: 3 posts
Bob: 2 posts
hints: In get_user_with_posts, use user = dict(self.users[user_id]) to copy the user.
Filter posts: user_posts = [p for p in self.posts if p['user_id'] == user_id]
For count_posts_per_user, loop over self.users and count matching posts for each.
Use sum(1 for p in self.posts if p['user_id'] == uid) for each user's count.
